tier_1_regimens.site: Resectable GIST with significant morbidity (Neoadjuvant setting)

biomarkers.requirement: Excluding PDGFRA exon 18 mutations that are insensitive to imatinib, such as D842V

tier_1_regimens.notes: Maximal response may require treatment for ≥6 months. Preoperative imatinib may prohibit accurate assessment of recurrence risk and should be considered only if surgical morbidity could be reduced by downstaging the tumor preoperatively. [5,6]

tier_1_regimens.site: Resectable GIST with significant morbidity (Neoadjuvant setting)

biomarkers.requirement: Insensitive to imatinib, including PDGFRA D842V

trial_data.OS: Median OS not reached (median follow-up 27.5 months)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Avapritinib is a selective inhibitor of KIT and PDGFRA. The approval was based on the NAVIGATOR phase I trial. Long-term data show an ORR of 91% with a median duration of response of 27.6 months. [1,2,7]

tier_1_regimens.site: Resectable GIST with significant morbidity (Neoadjuvant setting)

trial_data.ORR: 75% (adults and children with NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors, including GIST)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Larotrectinib is a selective inhibitor of TRK. It is FDA-approved for solid tumors with NTRK gene fusions. [8]

tier_1_regimens.site: Resectable GIST with significant morbidity (Neoadjuvant setting)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Entrectinib is a selective inhibitor of TRK. It is FDA-approved for solid tumors with NTRK gene fusions. [9]

tier_1_regimens.site: Resectable GIST with significant morbidity (Neoadjuvant setting)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Repotrectinib is a next-generation TRK inhibitor. [3]

tier_1_regimens.site: Resectable GIST with significant morbidity (Neoadjuvant setting)

biomarkers.requirement: Succinate dehydrogenase deficiency (detected by lack of expression of SDHB on IHC)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Sunitinib is a multitargeted TKI. SDH-deficient GIST may have a higher probability of response to sunitinib. Data from small retrospective studies suggest activity. [85,86]

tier_1_regimens.site: Resectable GIST with significant morbidity (Neoadjuvant setting)

biomarkers.requirement: Succinate dehydrogenase deficiency (detected by lack of expression of SDHB on IHC)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Regorafenib is a multikinase inhibitor with activity against VEGFR. It was reported to be clinically active against SDH-deficient GIST in a small phase II study. [88,89]

tier_1_regimens.site: Resectable GIST with significant morbidity (Neoadjuvant setting)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Dabrafenib is a BRAF inhibitor and trametinib is a MEK inhibitor. The combination is FDA-approved for BRAF V600E-mutated solid tumors, including GIST. [4]

biomarkers.requirement: Imatinib-sensitive mutation (data do not support routine use in GIST without mutation in KIT or with an imatinib-resistant mutation in PDGFRA)

trial_data.PFS: HR 0.6 (95% CI 0.43-0.75, P < .001) for recurrence-free survival

tier_1_regimens.notes: Adjuvant imatinib is recommended for patients with significant risk of recurrence, moderate or high risk (category 1 following complete resection with no preoperative imatinib). The optimal duration is unknown. Available data support use for high-risk disease for at least 3 years (based on overall survival benefit) or at least 6 years (based on disease-free survival benefit). [b,w]

biomarkers.requirement: Imatinib-sensitive mutations (excluding PDGFRA exon 18 mutations that are insensitive to imatinib)

trial_data.ORR: Objective responses in more than 50% of patients
trial_data.PFS: Estimated 9-year OS rate 35% for all patients (phase II); 10-year PFS rates 9.5% vs 9.2% (HR 0.91, P=.18) for 400 mg vs 800 mg (EORTC)
trial_data.OS: 10-year OS rates 19.4% vs 21.5% (HR 0.93, P=.31) for 400 mg vs 800 mg (EORTC)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Imatinib is the standard first-line treatment. Patients with KIT exon 9 mutations may benefit from dose escalation to 800 mg daily (400 mg BID). Patients with PDGFRA D842V mutations do not respond to imatinib. [5,6]

drugs.name: Sunitinib
drugs.schedule: daily for 4 weeks on, 2 weeks off (or continuous daily dosing 37.5 mg)

trial_data.ORR: PR in 14 patients (6.8%) and stable disease (≥22 weeks) in 36 patients (17.4%)
trial_data.PFS: Median time to progression 27.3 weeks vs 6.4 weeks (HR 0.33, P<.0001)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Sunitinib is a multitargeted TKI. SDH-deficient GIST may have a higher probability of response. For patients intolerant of second-line sunitinib, consider changing to ripretinib 150 mg daily. [11]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ripretinib 150 mg daily (Second-line alternative for sunitinib-intolerant)

biomarkers.requirement: Imatinib-sensitive GIST (if intolerant of second-line sunitinib)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ripretinib is a switch control kinase inhibitor of a broad spectrum of oncogenic and drug-resistant KIT and PDGFRA variants. Consider as an alternative for patients intolerant of sunitinib. [12]

drugs.name: Regorafenib
drugs.schedule: daily for 3 weeks on, 1 week off

trial_data.OS: HR for OS 0.77 (85% of placebo arm crossed over to regorafenib)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Regorafenib is a multikinase inhibitor. Patients with KIT exon 11 mutations or SDH-deficient GIST may derive a greater PFS benefit. [14]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ripretinib 150 mg daily (Fourth-line for imatinib-sensitive GIST)

biomarkers.requirement: Prior treatment with 3 or more kinase inhibitors, including imatinib

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ripretinib is approved for adults with advanced GIST who have received prior treatment with 3 or more kinase inhibitors, including imatinib. [15]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Avapritinib (First-line for PDGFRA exon 18 mutant GIST)

trial_data.OS: Median OS not reached (median follow-up 27.5 months)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Avapritinib is a selective inhibitor of KIT and PDGFRA. It is FDA-approved for unresectable or metastatic GIST with a PDGFRA mutation, including D842V. [1,2,7]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dasatinib (Other recommended for PDGFRA exon 18 mutant GIST)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Dasatinib is a dual SRC/ABL kinase inhibitor. It has shown in vitro activity against PDGFRA D842V and some clinical activity in a phase II study. [13]

trial_data.ORR: 75% (adults and children with NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors, including GIST)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Larotrectinib is FDA-approved for solid tumors with NTRK gene fusions. [8]
Entrectinib is FDA-approved for solid tumors with NTRK gene fusions. [9]
Repotrectinib is a next-generation TRK inhibitor. [3]

biomarkers.requirement: Succinate dehydrogenase deficiency (detected by lack of expression of SDHB on IHC)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Sunitinib is a multitargeted TKI with activity against VEGFR. Data from small retrospective studies suggest activity in SDH-deficient GIST. [85,86]

biomarkers.requirement: Succinate dehydrogenase deficiency (detected by lack of expression of SDHB on IHC)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Regorafenib is a multikinase inhibitor with activity against VEGFR. It was reported to be clinically active against SDH-deficient GIST in a small phase II study. [88,89]

biomarkers.requirement: Succinate dehydrogenase deficiency (detected by lack of expression of SDHB on IHC)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pazopanib is a TKI that targets VEGFR. A phase II study showed prolonged disease control in one patient with SDH-deficient GIST. [90,91]

biomarkers.requirement: Succinate dehydrogenase deficiency (detected by lack of expression of SDHB on IHC)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Binimetinib is a MEK inhibitor. The combination is being studied in SDH-deficient GIST. [10]
The combination is FDA-approved for BRAF V600E-mutated solid tumors, including GIST. [4]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dasatinib (Second-line for PDGFRA exon 18 mutant GIST after avapritinib)

biomarkers.requirement: Including PDGFRA D842V (resistant to avapritinib or imatinib)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Dasatinib is a dual SRC/ABL kinase inhibitor. It is recommended as a preferred second-line therapy for patients with PDGFRA exon 18 mutations whose disease has become resistant to avapritinib or imatinib. [13,133-135]
Consider repotrectinib as a second-line option for NTRK fusion-positive GIST if not previously given. [3]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy irrespective of mutation status
tier_1_regimens.notes: Consider combination immunotherapy for patients with TMB-H GIST based on limited data. [27]

tier_2_handbook.definition: Gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST) are the most common soft tissue sarcomas of the gastrointestinal (GI) tract, primarily arising from interstitial cells of Cajal or their precursors, which are pacemaker cells involved in GI motility [1,6]. Embryologically, these cells originate from the mesenchyme. Anatomically, GIST can develop anywhere along the GI tract, with the stomach being the most frequent site (approximately 60% of cases), followed by the small intestine (30%), duodenum (4%–5%), rectum (4%), esophagus (<1%), and colon or appendix (1%–2%) [6]. Rarely, GIST can occur in extraintestinal sites such as the omentum or peritoneum. Diagnosis relies on morphologic examination of tumor tissue, supported by immunohistochemical staining for CD117 (KIT protein, expressed in 95% of GIST), DOG1, and CD34 (expressed in about 70% of cases) [6,7]. Molecular genetic testing for mutations in the KIT or PDGFRA genes is essential for confirming diagnosis and guiding therapy, as these mutations define most GIST [9,10,11]. Clinical presentation varies; patients may experience abdominal discomfort, early satiety, GI bleeding due to ulceration, or an acute abdomen from tumor rupture, obstruction, or peritonitis [7]. GIST tends to be fragile, with a pseudocapsule, and metastasis most commonly occurs to the liver and peritoneum, while lymph node involvement is rare except in specific subtypes [6].

subtypes.name: KIT-mutant GIST
subtypes.description: The most common subtype, characterized by activating mutations in the KIT gene encoding a receptor tyrosine kinase. Mutations typically occur in exon 11 (juxtamembrane domain, most frequent) or exon 9 (extracellular domain), with rarer mutations in exons 13 or 17 [13,14]. KIT mutations lead to constitutive kinase activation, driving tumor growth.
subtypes.key_associations: KIT exon 11 mutations are associated with a better response to imatinib and higher risk of recurrence compared to exon 9 mutations [27,70]. KIT exon 9 mutations are more common in intestinal GIST and may require higher imatinib doses for optimal response [73,76].

subtypes.name: PDGFRA-mutant GIST
subtypes.description: Characterized by mutations in the PDGFRA gene, another receptor tyrosine kinase. Mutations predominantly affect exon 18 (tyrosine kinase domain), such as D842V, with rarer mutations in exon 12 (juxtamembrane domain) or exon 14 [15]. These mutations result in autonomous signaling.
subtypes.key_associations: PDGFRA exon 18 mutations, particularly D842V, are imatinib-insensitive but respond to avapritinib [79,81]. Most other PDGFRA mutations are sensitive to imatinib [11,15]. This subtype is more common in gastric GIST [13].

subtypes.name: SDH-deficient GIST
subtypes.description: Also known as wild-type GIST lacking KIT or PDGFRA mutations, these tumors have functional inactivation of the succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) complex due to mutations in SDH subunits (e.g., SDHB, SDHC, SDHD) or epigenetic silencing [16,18,19]. Loss of SDHB expression by immunohistochemistry is a hallmark.
subtypes.key_associations: Often arise in the stomach, occur in younger individuals, and may have a more indolent but unpredictable behavior, with potential for lymph node metastasis [83]. They are typically resistant to imatinib but may respond to sunitinib or regorafenib [85,86]. Associated with germline SDH mutations and risk of paraganglioma [GIST-B].

subtypes.name: NF1-associated GIST
subtypes.description: Arise in patients with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) syndrome, caused by inactivating mutations in the NF1 gene, leading to loss of neurofibromin and activation of RAS signaling [101].
subtypes.frequency: Rare; a small subset of GIST cases [101,102].
subtypes.key_associations: Typically occur in the small intestine, may be multifocal, and often have an indolent biology. Should be tested for concurrent KIT or PDGFRA mutations [GIST-B]. Data on TKI efficacy are limited [GIST-B].

subtypes.name: BRAF-mutant GIST
subtypes.description: Characterized by activating mutations in the BRAF gene, most commonly V600E, which activates the MAPK pathway.
subtypes.frequency: Occurs in a small subset of GIST, particularly intestinal high-risk tumors lacking KIT/PDGFRA mutations [21,22].
subtypes.key_associations: May respond to BRAF-directed therapies like dabrafenib/trametinib [4].

subtypes.name: NTRK-fusion positive GIST
subtypes.description: Defined by gene fusions involving NTRK genes (e.g., NTRK1, NTRK2, NTRK3), leading to TRK kinase activation.
subtypes.frequency: Rare; a small percentage of GIST cases [93,94,95].
subtypes.key_associations: Responsive to TRK inhibitors such as larotrectinib, entrectinib, or repotrectinib [8,9,3].

subtypes.name: Histologic subtypes
subtypes.description: Based on morphology, GIST can be classified as spindle cell type (most common, with elongated cells), epithelioid type (round to polygonal cells), or mixed type. Spindle cell GIST is typical for KIT-mutant tumors, while epithelioid may be more common in PDGFRA-mutant or SDH-deficient tumors [6,12].
subtypes.frequency: Spindle cell type predominates in most GIST.
subtypes.key_associations: Histology alone does not determine prognosis; molecular subtyping is critical for therapy [7].

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular pathogenesis of GIST is driven by constitutive activation of tyrosine kinase signaling pathways, primarily through oncogenic mutations in KIT or PDGFRA receptor tyrosine kinase genes [1,10]. Approximately 80% of GIST harbor mutations in the KIT gene, which encodes a transmembrane receptor involved in cell growth and survival [9,10]. Most KIT mutations occur in exon 11 (juxtamembrane domain), leading to ligand-independent activation; mutations in exon 9 (extracellular domain) are less common and often associated with intestinal GIST [13]. Rarely, mutations in exons 13 or 17 (tyrosine kinase domain) occur [14]. About 5%–10% of GIST have mutations in the PDGFRA gene, a related receptor tyrosine kinase, with the majority affecting exon 18 (tyrosine kinase domain), such as the D842V mutation [11,15]. These mutations result in gain-of-function, driving tumor proliferation via downstream pathways like RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT. In 10%–15% of GIST, neither KIT nor PDGFRA mutations are detectable (wild-type GIST); the majority of these have functional inactivation of the succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) complex due to mutations in SDH subunits (SDHA, SDHB, SDHC, SDHD) or epigenetic silencing, leading to metabolic reprogramming and tumorigenesis [16,18,19]. SDH-deficient GIST often arise in younger patients and have a distinct clinical behavior [83]. Other rare genomic events include BRAF V600E mutations (activating the MAPK pathway), inactivating NF1 mutations (affecting RAS regulation), and gene fusions involving NTRK or FGFR, which can serve as alternative drivers [21,93,101]. Chromosomal abnormalities, such as deletions of chromosome 14q and 22q, are frequently observed and may contribute to loss of tumor suppressor genes [7]. These molecular alterations dictate response to targeted therapies, such as tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), and are essential for risk stratification and treatment planning [GIST-B].
tier_2_handbook.epidemiology: The annual incidence of gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST) in the United States is estimated to be between 0.68 and 0.78 per 100,000 population, based on population-based studies [2,3,4,5]. Mortality data are less consistently reported, but survival rates vary by stage and risk stratification; for example, the 5-year survival rate for localized GIST is high after resection, but drops significantly for metastatic disease [49,50]. Trends suggest a potential increase in incidence over time, possibly due to improved diagnostic methods and awareness, though this is not explicitly detailed in the source [2]. Demographically, GIST typically affects adults, with a median age at diagnosis around 60–65 years; it is slightly more common in males than females in some series, but the source does not provide specific sex ratios [6]. Racial and ethnic disparities are not mentioned in the provided text; however, incidence may vary geographically, but no specific SEER or GLOBOCAN numbers are given beyond the U.S. estimates. Primary site distribution is well-documented: stomach accounts for 60% of cases, small intestine for 30%, duodenum for 4%–5%, rectum for 4%, esophagus for <1%, and colon or appendix for 1%–2%, with rare extraintestinal occurrences [6]. These sites have different prognostic implications, as gastric GIST tend to be more indolent compared to small intestinal or rectal GIST, which are more aggressive [6,24,25].

risk_factors.detail: Germline mutations in the NF1 gene predispose to the development of GIST, often multifocal and arising in the small intestine. NF1-associated GIST may have a more indolent course but require genetic counseling and testing [101,102].
risk_factors.strength: Strong association based on clinical and genetic studies; evidence supports that NF1 loss contributes to tumorigenesis [101].

risk_factors.detail: An autosomal dominant disorder caused by germline mutations in SDH subunit genes (e.g., SDHB, SDHC, SDHD), leading to SDH-deficient GIST and a predisposition to paragangliomas [16,18].
risk_factors.strength: Strong evidence linking SDH mutations to GIST development; germline testing is recommended for patients with SDH-deficient GIST [GIST-B].

risk_factors.detail: Rare familial cases with germline KIT or PDGFRA mutations, often presenting with multiple GIST and other features like mastocytosis [GIST-B].
risk_factors.strength: Established through genetic studies; germline testing is considered for patients with family history or multifocal disease [GIST-B].

risk_factors.detail: Most GIST occur sporadically due to somatic mutations in KIT or PDGFRA genes, not inherited. Risk factors for these mutations are not well-defined, and environmental factors are not clearly identified in the source.
risk_factors.strength: Common mechanism; evidence from molecular studies supports somatic mutation as the primary driver [9,10].

risk_factors.detail: Loss of SDH function, whether from mutations or epigenetic silencing, is a key risk factor for a subset of GIST, particularly in younger individuals [16,19].
risk_factors.strength: Well-supported by molecular and pathological studies; SDH-deficient GIST represent a distinct entity [16].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST) are the most common soft tissue sarcoma of the gastrointestinal (GI) tract. Patients with a suspected GIST may present with a variety of symptoms, which may include early satiety, abdominal discomfort due to pain or swelling, intraperitoneal hemorrhage, GI bleeding, or fatigue related to anemia. Some patients may present with an acute abdomen, which requires immediate medical attention [MS-2]. GIST can arise anywhere along the GI tract, but stomach (60%) and small intestine (30%) are the most common primary sites. Duodenum (4%–5%) and rectum (4%) are less common, and only a small number of cases have been reported in the esophagus (<1%) and colon and appendix (1%–2%) [6].

symptoms.name: Early satiety
symptoms.detail: May occur due to mass effect, especially with gastric GIST.

symptoms.name: Abdominal discomfort/pain
symptoms.detail: Can be due to tumor size, local growth, or swelling.

symptoms.name: GI bleeding
symptoms.detail: May present as melena or hematemesis; can be overt or occult leading to anemia.

symptoms.name: Intraperitoneal hemorrhage
symptoms.detail: Acute event, often due to tumor rupture.

symptoms.name: Fatigue
symptoms.detail: Often related to chronic blood loss and subsequent anemia.

symptoms.name: Acute abdomen
symptoms.detail: Resulting from tumor rupture, GI obstruction, or peritonitis-like pain.
symptoms.frequency: Less common but requires immediate attention [MS-2]

signs.name: Palpable abdominal mass
signs.detail: May be discovered on physical examination, especially with larger tumors.

atypical_or_missed: Incidentally discovered small (<2 cm) gastric GIST on endoscopy performed for other reasons [GIST-1].
Tumors may be asymptomatic and found during workup for unrelated conditions.
Presentation with symptoms mimicking benign ulcer disease or functional GI disorders, which may delay diagnosis.

red_flags: Acute abdomen due to tumor rupture, GI obstruction, or peritonitis-like pain requiring immediate medical attention [MS-2].
Evidence of peritoneal spread or ascites on imaging.

diagnostic.test: Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) with fine-needle aspiration biopsy (EUS-FNAB) or core needle biopsy (EUS-CNB)
diagnostic.rationale: EUS-FNAB is favored over percutaneous biopsy for primary lesions due to lower risk of peritoneal seeding. Biopsy is necessary to confirm diagnosis before preoperative therapy [GIST-A, MS-2].
diagnostic.key_findings: Tissue for morphologic diagnosis and ancillary testing (IHC, molecular).

diagnostic.rationale: Primary imaging modality to characterize abdominal mass, evaluate extent, and stage disease. Performed for workup, response assessment, and surveillance [GIST-F, MS-4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Tumor size, location, presence of metastases (liver, peritoneum).

diagnostic.test: MRI abdomen/pelvis with and without contrast
diagnostic.rationale: Alternative or adjunct to CT, especially if contrast allergy or for better soft tissue definition [GIST-F].
diagnostic.key_findings: Similar to CT; may provide superior detail for rectal or pelvic tumors.

diagnostic.rationale: Can be used to assess early response to TKI therapy (after 2–4 weeks), clarify ambiguous CT/MRI findings, or evaluate complex metastatic disease before surgery. Should not substitute for diagnostic CT [GIST-F, MS-4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Metabolic activity; decrease indicates response to TKI.

diagnostic.rationale: Direct visualization and potential biopsy of GI lesions if not previously done [GIST-1].

staging.rationale: Recommended at baseline for unresectable or metastatic disease to evaluate for pulmonary metastases [GIST-1, GIST-F].

biomarkers.rationale: Standard diagnostic marker; expressed in ~95% of GIST [MS-2, GIST-A].
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis for all suspected GIST.

biomarkers.rationale: Useful for diagnosis, especially in KIT-negative GIST or difficult cases [MS-3, GIST-A].
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis, especially if KIT-negative or ambiguous.

biomarkers.rationale: To detect SDH-deficient GIST (lack of SDHB expression) in KIT/PDGFRA wild-type tumors [GIST-B, MS-3].
biomarkers.when: All GIST lacking KIT or PDGFRA mutations.

biomarkers.test: Molecular testing for KIT and PDGFRA mutations
biomarkers.rationale: Predicts response to TKIs. Required before initiating preoperative or systemic TKI therapy [GIST-B, MS-9].
biomarkers.when: When medical therapy (TKI) is being considered [GIST-1].

biomarkers.rationale: To identify alternative driver mutations (e.g., BRAF, NF1, NTRK, FGFR fusions) in tumors lacking KIT/PDGFRA mutations or for comprehensive profiling [GIST-B].
biomarkers.when: All GIST lacking KIT or PDGFRA mutations, or for identifying targetable alterations.

do_not_miss: Molecular testing (KIT/PDGFRA) before starting neoadjuvant or systemic TKI therapy [GIST-1, GIST-B].
SDHB IHC in KIT/PDGFRA wild-type GIST to identify SDH-deficient tumors [GIST-B].
Referral to a sarcoma expert center for complex or unusual histopathologic features [GIST-A, MS-2].
avoid: Percutaneous transperitoneal biopsy for primary lesions due to risk of peritoneal seeding; favor endoscopic transmural biopsy [GIST-A].
Incisional biopsy before or during operation [GIST-C].
Risk stratification based on mitotic rate alone in small biopsies or after neoadjuvant therapy, as it may be inaccurate [GIST-A].

staging.system: American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) Staging System, 8th Edition (2017) [ST-1].

t_stages.description: Tumor 2 cm or less in greatest dimension.
Tumor more than 2 cm but not more than 5 cm in greatest dimension.
Tumor more than 5 cm but not more than 10 cm in greatest dimension.
Tumor more than 10 cm in greatest dimension.

n_stages.description: No regional lymph node metastasis or unknown lymph node status.

stage_groups.criteria:
Stage IA: T1 or T2, N0, M0, Low mitotic rate.
Stage IB: T3, N0, M0, Low mitotic rate.
Stage II: T1 or T2, N0, M0, High mitotic rate; OR T4, N0, M0, Low mitotic rate.
Stage IIIA: T3, N0, M0, High mitotic rate.
Stage IIIB: T4, N0, M0, High mitotic rate.
Stage IV: Any T, N1, M0, Any mitotic rate; OR Any T, Any N, M1, Any mitotic rate.
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Prognostic groups based on tumor size, nodal status, metastasis, and mitotic rate (Low: ≤5 mitoses per 50 HPF; High: >5 mitoses per 50 HPF).
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Staging guides prognosis and treatment decisions (e.g., adjuvant therapy for high-risk).

stage_groups.group: Small Intestinal GIST (also used for esophagus, colorectal, mesenteric, peritoneal)
stage_groups.criteria:
Stage I: T1 or T2, N0, M0, Low mitotic rate.
Stage II: T3, N0, M0, Low mitotic rate.
Stage IIIA: T1 or T4, N0, M0, High mitotic rate; OR T4, N0, M0, Low mitotic rate.
Stage IIIB: T2, T3, or T4, N0, M0, High mitotic rate.
Stage IV: Any T, N1, M0, Any mitotic rate; OR Any T, Any N, M1, Any mitotic rate.
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Similar prognostic grouping but with different stage assignments reflecting more aggressive biology of non-gastric GIST.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Staging guides prognosis and treatment decisions.

staging_pearls: Gastric GIST are generally more indolent than small intestinal GIST [GIST-A, MS-3].
Risk stratification for recurrence after resection also incorporates tumor size, mitotic rate, and anatomic location (gastric vs. non-gastric) [GIST-A].
Tumor rupture during surgery or preoperatively is associated with a much higher risk of recurrence and should be considered metastatic disease [GIST-3].
The mitotic rate should be measured in the most proliferative area of the tumor and reported per 50 high-power fields (HPFs), equivalent to 5 mm² of tissue [GIST-A].
SDH-deficient GIST are more unpredictable in behavior and are not well stratified by standard risk tables [GIST-A].

prognosis.overall: Gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST) have a variable prognosis influenced by tumor size, mitotic rate, anatomic site, and molecular profile. The annual incidence in the United States is estimated between 0.68 and 0.78 per 100,000 [2-5]. GIST can arise anywhere in the gastrointestinal tract, with the stomach (60%) and small intestine (30%) being the most common sites [6]. Approximately 80% of GIST harbor KIT mutations and 5%-10% have PDGFRA mutations, which are targetable by tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), significantly improving outcomes [1]. With surgery and adjuvant therapy, many patients achieve long-term disease control, but recurrence remains a risk, especially in high-risk disease [53,56]. The natural history has been transformed by imatinib, which induces objective responses in over 50% of patients with advanced disease [38,43].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Approximately 50% after complete resection without adjuvant therapy [49-51]. With adjuvant imatinib, recurrence-free survival is significantly improved; in the ACOSOG Z9001 trial, the hazard ratio (HR) for recurrence was 0.6 (95% CI, 0.43–0.75; P < .001) [53,54].
by_stage.context: Risk stratification using tumor size, mitotic rate, and site predicts recurrence. For gastric GIST, metastasis rates are 0% for tumors ≤2 cm, 1.9% for 2-5 cm with low mitoses, and up to 86% for >10 cm with high mitoses [24]. For non-gastric GIST, rates are higher: 50-54% for ≤2 cm with high mitoses and 71-90% for >10 cm with high mitoses [25].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Long-term survival is limited; in the B2222 study, the estimated 9-year overall survival (OS) was 35% for all patients [43]. With first-line imatinib, median OS is significantly improved compared to historical controls.
by_stage.context: Mutational status impacts prognosis: KIT exon 11 mutations associate with better response to imatinib than exon 9 mutations [70,73,74]. PDGFRA D842V mutations confer resistance to imatinib but respond to avapritinib [79,81]. SDH-deficient GIST are more unpredictable and often resistant to imatinib [83,84].

prognostic_factors: Tumor size and mitotic rate are primary pathologic features for risk stratification [24,25].
Anatomic location: gastric GIST are indolent, especially if ≤2 cm; small intestinal GIST are more aggressive; rectal GIST are very aggressive [6,139].
Mutation type: KIT exon 9 mutations predict lower response to imatinib 400 mg daily but benefit from higher doses [73,76]. PDGFRA D842V mutations are imatinib-insensitive [11,15].
Response to TKI therapy: Patients with objective response or stable disease have better outcomes [38,43].
Completeness of resection and tumor rupture: R0 resection is the goal; tumor rupture significantly increases recurrence risk [v,52].
Sex: Female sex may associate with better PFS and OS in some studies [27].
Genotype: KIT exon 11 deletions and non-gastric site are associated with poorer disease-free survival [26].
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis in GIST has been revolutionized by targeted therapies. Before imatinib, advanced GIST had poor outcomes. Now, with surgery and TKI therapy, many patients achieve long-term disease control. However, resistance develops, and prognosis varies based on molecular profiles and treatment sequencing.

disease_related.management: Systemic therapy with TKIs; surgery or interventional procedures for limited disease [GIST-4, GIST-5]. Liver and peritoneal metastases are most common; lymph node metastases are rare except in SDH-deficient GIST [MS-2].
Emergency surgery; considered as metastatic disease due to high recurrence risk [GIST-3, GIST-C]. Tumor rupture is a poor prognostic factor [v,52].
Surgical intervention [GIST-C]. May present as acute abdomen requiring immediate attention [MS-2].

treatment_related.chemotherapy: GIST are generally resistant to conventional chemotherapy; no standard regimens are recommended [MS-2].
treatment_related.immunotherapy: Limited data; ipilimumab plus nivolumab is considered for tumor mutational burden-high (TMB-H) GIST [27].

targeted_therapy.imatinib: Common adverse events include fluid retention, diarrhea, nausea, fatigue, muscle cramps, abdominal pain, and rash. Serious toxicities: liver function test abnormalities (<5%), lung toxicity, neutropenia, GI bleeding, and cardiotoxicity (grade 3/4 in 8.2%) [59,60]. Management involves dose adjustments and supportive care.
targeted_therapy.sunitinib: Common toxicities: fatigue, nausea, vomiting, hematologic toxicities, diarrhea, hand-foot skin reaction (HFSR), hypertension, hypothyroidism, and cardiotoxicity. HFSR occurs in up to 20% of patients [65]; hypertension in 28% and hypothyroidism in 12% [68]. Management includes dose interruptions/reductions, emollients for HFSR, antihypertensives, and thyroid hormone replacement.
targeted_therapy.regorafenib: Similar TKI-related toxicities, with diarrhea, HFSR, and hypertension being common [88].
targeted_therapy.ripretinib: Common adverse events include alopecia, diarrhea, nausea, and fatigue; serious potential for secondary malignancies [117].

treatment_related.surgery: Risks include tumor rupture, spillage, bleeding, infection, and anastomotic leak. Avoidance of pseudocapsule violation is critical. Minimally invasive approaches are selected for favorable locations [GIST-C, MS-4].
treatment_related.radiation: Palliative radiotherapy for symptomatic lesions, especially bone metastases; category 2B recommendation [GIST-5]. May cause local tissue damage.

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care in GIST is integral to maintaining quality of life during long-term TKI therapy. It involves proactive monitoring and management of treatment-related toxicities, nutritional support, and psychosocial care. A multidisciplinary team approach is recommended [GIST-1].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Patients with GIST, especially those with GI involvement, may experience weight loss, anorexia, or malnutrition. Nutritional assessment and dietetic support are recommended, with enteral or parenteral nutrition if needed to maintain adequate intake and prevent cachexia.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Nausea and vomiting are common with imatinib and sunitinib. Antiemetics such as ondansetron or prochlorperazine may be used as needed, based on the emetogenic potential of the TKI. Prophylactic antiemetics are not standard but can be considered for patients with severe symptoms.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (GCSF) is not routinely recommended but may be considered for severe neutropenia, particularly with sunitinib or combination therapies. Close monitoring of complete blood counts is advised.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis is not specifically addressed in the guideline; standard cancer care guidelines should be followed for high-risk patients, such as those with immobilization or other risk factors.
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain from tumor bulk, bone metastases, or treatment-related effects should be managed with analgesics, including opioids if necessary. Palliative radiotherapy is an option for symptomatic lesions [GIST-5]. A multimodal approach incorporating pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic methods is recommended.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: The chronic nature of GIST and long-term TKI therapy can impact mental health. Referral to counseling, support groups, and palliative care services is encouraged to address anxiety, depression, and quality of life concerns.
supportive_care.dental_care: Oral health is important during TKI therapy; patients should maintain good dental hygiene and report any oral ulcers or mucositis. Dental evaluations prior to starting therapy may be beneficial to prevent complications.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After complete resection without neoadjuvant therapy, history and physical (H&P) and imaging (CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast or MRI) are recommended every 3–6 months for 5 years, then annually. After 10 years, surveillance should be individualized [GIST-3, GIST-F]. For low-risk tumors, less frequent surveillance may be acceptable [GIST-F].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Surveillance aims to detect recurrence early, as treatment with TKIs is effective for recurrent or metastatic disease. Recurrence patterns indicate that most recurrences occur within the first 5 years, but late recurrences are possible, necessitating long-term monitoring.
late_effects_screening: Monitor for cardiac toxicity with imatinib and sunitinib: echocardiography or left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) assessment if symptoms arise [60,67].
Screen for hypothyroidism with sunitinib every 3–6 months via thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) testing [68].
Assess for other TKI-related effects such as skin changes, hypertension, and fatigue during follow-up visits.
Evaluate for bone health in patients on long-term TKI therapy, as some may be at risk for osteoporosis.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Median time to recurrence after resection of high-risk GIST is about 2 years [49-51]. Recurrence can be locoregional or metastatic, commonly to the liver and peritoneum [MS-2]. Risk of recurrence is influenced by tumor size, mitotic rate, site, and mutation status [6,24,25].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: H&P every 3–6 months for the first 5 years post-resection, then annually [GIST-3].
For high-risk disease, more frequent imaging may be required [GIST-F].
For unresectable or metastatic disease on TKI therapy, H&P and imaging every 3–6 months to assess response [GIST-4].
imaging_strategy: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast and/or MRI abdomen/pelvis with and without contrast every 3–6 months for 5 years, then annually [GIST-F].
FDG-PET/CT may be used to clarify ambiguous CT/MRI findings or to assess response to TKI therapy; it can indicate TKI efficacy after 2–4 weeks [GIST-F, MS-4].
Chest imaging (x-ray or CT) should be considered for unresectable or metastatic disease [GIST-F].
For very small gastric GIST <2 cm, periodic endoscopic or radiographic surveillance may be considered [GIST-1].
laboratory_monitoring: Not explicitly specified in the guideline, but for patients on sunitinib, monitor TSH every 3–6 months [68].
Liver function tests for imatinib toxicity, especially in the first few months [59].
Complete blood counts periodically for hematologic toxicities, particularly with sunitinib or regorafenib.
Serum chemistry panels to monitor electrolytes and renal function.
supportive_follow_up: Monitoring for TKI side effects and management as per toxicity guidelines, with dose modifications as needed.
Psychosocial support and symptom management to enhance quality of life.
Referral to survivorship programs for long-term care and monitoring of late effects.

special_situations.key_message: These tumors lack KIT/PDGFRA mutations, are often gastric, and occur in younger patients. They may metastasize and involve lymph nodes, but tend to grow slowly. Imatinib has limited efficacy.
special_situations.management: Consider sunitinib or regorafenib. Refer for germline SDH mutation testing and screen for paraganglioma with 24-hour urine catecholamines before surgery [GIST-B, GIST-E]. Multivisceral resection is generally not recommended for multifocal disease.
special_situations.evidence: Retrospective data suggest sunitinib activity in SDH-deficient GIST [85,86]; regorafenib also has activity [89].

special_situations.key_message: These mutations are insensitive to imatinib but respond to avapritinib.
special_situations.management: Avapritinib is preferred first-line for unresectable/metastatic disease. Dasatinib is an option after avapritinib resistance [GIST-E].
special_situations.evidence: NAVIGATOR trial showed overall response rate (ORR) 91% with avapritinib in PDGFRA D842V GIST [81,82].

special_situations.key_message: Actionable fusion that responds to TRK inhibitors.
special_situations.management: Larotrectinib or entrectinib are preferred; repotrectinib is a category 2B option [GIST-E].
special_situations.evidence: Larotrectinib ORR 75% in TRK fusion-positive cancers [98]; entrectinib ORR 57% in adults [99].

special_situations.key_message: Rare mutation; combination therapy with dabrafenib/trametinib is approved.
special_situations.evidence: ROAR trial showed activity in BRAF V600E rare cancers [4].

special_situations.key_message: Typically small bowel, multifocal, indolent biology.
special_situations.management: Refer for genetic counseling. Limited data on TKIs; clinical trial participation [GIST-B].

clinical_pearls: GIST are the most common sarcomas of the GI tract, driven by KIT or PDGFRA mutations [1,2].
Risk stratification for localized GIST is based on tumor size, mitotic rate, and anatomic site, using guidelines from Miettinen et al. [6,24,25].
Mutation testing is essential to guide therapy: imatinib for KIT/PDGFRA mutations, avapritinib for PDGFRA D842V, and specific therapies for NTRK fusions, BRAF mutations, etc. [GIST-B].
Surgery is the primary treatment for localized GIST, aiming for complete resection without tumor rupture. Lymphadenectomy is usually not required [GIST-C].
Adjuvant imatinib is recommended for intermediate- or high-risk resected GIST for at least 3 years, with data supporting 6 years for high-risk based on disease-free survival benefit [53,56,57].
For advanced GIST, imatinib is first-line; sunitinib second-line; regorafenib third-line; ripretinib fourth-line, based on clinical trial evidence [61,116,118].
SDH-deficient GIST are imatinib-resistant but may respond to sunitinib or regorafenib; germline testing is indicated [GIST-B].
Tumor response to TKIs is assessed by CT or MRI; Choi criteria may be more sensitive than RECIST for GIST [29], but RECIST is also used in practice.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumors
guidelines_resources.notes: Version 1.2026, updated January 13, 2026. Provides evidence-based recommendations for diagnosis, staging, treatment, and surveillance.

key_trials.full_name: Phase II study of imatinib in advanced GIST
key_trials.population: Patients with metastatic or unresectable KIT-positive GIST
key_trials.key_result: Objective response in >50% of patients; estimated 9-year OS 35% [43].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Long-term disease control; low tumor bulk at baseline predicted longer TTP and improved OS.
key_trials.practice_change: Established imatinib as standard therapy for advanced GIST.

key_trials.full_name: Randomized trial of imatinib at two dose levels in advanced GIST
key_trials.intervention: Imatinib 400 mg daily vs 800 mg daily
key_trials.key_result: No significant difference in PFS or OS at 10.9 years; HR for PFS 0.91 (95% CI, 0.79–1.04; P = .18); OS HR 0.93 (95% CI, 0.80–1.07; P = .31) [44].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Response rates similar; higher dose associated with more side effects.
key_trials.practice_change: Standard dose imatinib 400 mg daily remains first-line.

key_trials.full_name: Phase III randomized intergroup trial of imatinib at two dose levels
key_trials.intervention: Imatinib 400 mg daily vs 800 mg daily
key_trials.key_result: No statistical difference in PFS or OS; median OS 55 vs 51 months [42].

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant imatinib for resected primary GIST
key_trials.population: Patients with primary localized GIST ≥3 cm after complete resection
key_trials.intervention: Imatinib 400 mg daily for 1 year vs placebo
key_trials.key_result: Significant improvement in RFS; HR 0.6 (95% CI, 0.43–0.75; P < .001); OS not significantly different [53,54].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Benefit in intermediate and high-risk tumors.
key_trials.practice_change: Established adjuvant imatinib for resected GIST.

key_trials.full_name: Randomized trial of adjuvant imatinib for 1 vs 3 years
key_trials.key_result: Longer duration improved RFS; 5-year RFS 71.1% vs 52.3% (P < .001); 5-year OS 91.9% vs 85.3% (P = .036) [56,57].
key_trials.practice_change: Extended adjuvant therapy for high-risk GIST.

key_trials.full_name: Phase III trial of regorafenib in GIST after imatinib and sunitinib
key_trials.population: Metastatic or unresectable GIST progressing on imatinib and sunitinib
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 4.8 vs 0.9 months (P < .0001); DCR 53% vs 9% [116].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: HR for OS 0.77 with 85% crossover to regorafenib.

key_trials.full_name: Phase III trial of ripretinib in advanced GIST
key_trials.population: Advanced GIST after imatinib, sunitinib, and regorafenib
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Dose escalation to 150 mg BID upon progression showed median OS 18.4 months [119].

key_trials.full_name: Phase I trial of avapritinib in PDGFRA D842V GIST
key_trials.population: Unresectable or metastatic GIST with PDGFRA D842V mutation
key_trials.key_result: ORR 91%; median duration of response 27.6 months [81,82].
key_trials.practice_change: Avapritinib approved for PDGFRA D842V GIST.
